commitMessage: Freeze on death showing the loss reason until a key is pressed

topics.title: Freeze game on death showing loss reason until key pressed
topics.trigger: When a snake died, the game immediately jumped to the Game Over screen, hiding the board entirely. Players never saw how they lost â€” the fatal collision wasn't even drawn on the board.
topics.response:
- Four death sites in `src/main.ts` (`updateSinglePlayer` collision, `updateSinglePlayer` starvation, `updateTwoPlayer` collision, `updateTwoPlayer` starvation) were rewired to call a new `beginDeathFreeze()` method instead of calling the end-game functions directly. Each site captures the fatal head cell coordinates and composes a human-readable loss message before entering the freeze. The existing `endGame()` and `endGameTwoPlayer()` functions are passed as deferred callbacks, so all winner resolution, high-score saving, and leaderboard logic runs unchanged when the freeze ends.
- `beginDeathFreeze()` stops the game loop, calls `draw()` to render the frozen board, and shows a pulsing `#death-banner` overlay (added to `index.html` inside the canvas wrapper, styled in `src/style.css`). The `draw()` method was extended to render an amber highlight block on top of the fatal cell(s) when the freeze is active; wall deaths clamp the marker to the last valid head position with a sliver drawn past the border edge.
- An acknowledgement branch was added at the top of `handleKeyPress` in `src/main.ts` that intercepts all keys during the freeze. Camera control keys (rotate, auto-rotate toggle, overhead, perspective) fall through to their normal handlers so the player can inspect the collision from any angle; every other key dismisses the freeze and runs the deferred end-game callback.
topics.decisions:
- **Deferred callback over rewriting end-game functions**: Rather than splitting or duplicating the end-game logic, the freeze inserts itself as a step in front of the existing functions by storing them as callbacks. This kept all the winner/leaderboard/high-score code untouched and made the change low-risk â€” the freeze is purely additive, and the finalize step is identical to what ran before.
- **Camera keys fall through during freeze**: The initial implementation dismissed the freeze on any key press. After review, rotate, overhead, and perspective keys were whitelisted to fall through, turning the frozen board into an inspectable "death cam." This required distinguishing between "view" keys and "intent to continue" keys, but the whitelist is small and the UX benefit â€” being able to spin the board to see exactly how you died â€” was considered worth the added complexity.
- **DOM overlay banner over canvas text**: The loss reason is shown as a DOM element overlaid on the canvas rather than drawn with canvas text. This mirrors the existing starvation-warning pattern already in the codebase, keeps the canvas rendering code free of text layout concerns, and means the banner inherits CSS animation and accessibility attributes without extra work.

recap:
When a snake dies, the game board now stays visible and frozen rather than immediately switching away. The fatal collision is highlighted on the board in an amber block, and a pulsing banner names exactly how the snake lost â€” hitting a wall, running into itself, crashing into the opponent, or starving. In two-player mode, the banner describes each snake's fate individually, and head-on collisions get their own message.

The freeze persists until the player presses any key, at which point the normal Game Over screen appears with scores, high-score entry, and leaderboard options all intact. During the freeze, the camera controls remain fully active: players can rotate the board, toggle overhead view, and adjust perspective to inspect the collision from any angle before moving on.
